_pass: Pass 1 + worked examples v2 + display math + LA trim + apps + extra exercises
title: Surveys and the margin of error
deck: The chapter closer. A sample of size n estimates a population proportion with a margin of error of roughly 1/√n. Shrinking the margin requires quadrupling the sample.
objectives: Compute the margin of error for a sample proportion at 95% confidence; read a poll headline (e.g., “45%, ±3%”) as a confidence interval; explain why quadrupling the sample size halves the margin of error.

content.text: Every poll headline in modern public life carries a tiny number that almost nobody reads: the margin of error. “45% of Americans favor the policy, ±3%” is a statistical claim with two halves. The 45% is what the pollsters measured in their sample; the ±3% is the size of the interval inside which the true population percentage almost certainly sits. The pollster who interviewed 1,000 people did not actually find out what all 150 million voters think; they made an inferential leap, and the ± is the honesty tax on that leap.

This section closes the chapter by developing the math behind that margin of error. The formula is surprisingly simple — for a sample proportion at 95% confidence, the margin of error is approximately one over the square root of the sample size — and it has a useful consequence: cutting the margin in half requires quadrupling the sample. Once you can compute and interpret a margin of error, you can read any poll, any clinical trial, any market-research headline with the same fluency a statistician brings to the same numbers.
5.6.1  Sample proportion and the rule of thumb

Suppose a pollster interviews n people from a population and finds that a fraction p̂ (the “sample proportion,” pronounced “p-hat”) holds a particular opinion. The unknown population proportion p is what we are really after; p̂ is our best estimate of it, but it is almost certainly not exactly equal to p. The question is: how confident can we be that p lies inside some specified interval around p̂? At the 95% confidence level, an excellent rule of thumb gives a clean answer:

Definition 5.8. The margin of error (MOE) for a sample proportion at 95% confidence is approximately 1 / √n, where n is the sample size. The 95% confidence interval is p̂ ± MOE; we say we are “95% confident” the true population proportion p lies inside this interval.

For a sample of n = 1,000, the margin of error is 1/√1000 ≈ 0.0316 ≈ 3%. For n = 100, it is 1/√100 = 0.10 = 10%. For n = 10,000, it is 1/√10000 = 0.01 = 1%. The pattern is the source of the rule's most useful consequence: to halve the margin of error, you must quadruple the sample, because the square root of 4 is 2.

content.caption: Figure 5.6. The margin of error for a sample proportion at 95% confidence is approximately 1/√n. The curve falls steeply at first and flattens out: each new doubling-then-doubling of n halves the margin, but the absolute gain shrinks quickly past n = 1,000.

content.text: The interval p̂ ± MOE is called the 95% confidence interval for the population proportion. The phrase “we are 95% confident” has a precise technical meaning that is easy to misinterpret. It does not mean that there is a 95% chance the true population proportion lies inside the specific interval computed from this particular sample; under the standard frequentist interpretation, the population proportion is a fixed (unknown) number, not a random variable. The correct interpretation is that the procedure used to construct the interval has a 95% long-run success rate: if many samples were drawn and a 95% confidence interval were computed from each, about 95% of those intervals would contain the true population proportion.

Margin noteThe 95% number is conventional, not mandatory. Other common confidence levels are 90% (smaller MOE, weaker guarantee) and 99% (larger MOE, stronger guarantee). The 95% level corresponds to approximately two standard deviations of the sampling distribution — the 1.96 that appears in the more precise margin-of-error formula MOE = 1.96 · √(p(1−p)/n). The simpler 1/√n rule of thumb is an upper bound on the more careful formula, accurate enough for most purposes.
The ± on every poll headline is not optional fine print. It is the inferential half of the statistical claim, and without it the headline cannot be properly read.

Common mistakeTreating two poll percentages whose intervals overlap as significantly different. If candidate A is at 48%, ±3% and candidate B is at 45%, ±3%, the two confidence intervals are [45, 51] and [42, 48] — overlapping. A statistician would say the race is “within the margin of error” or “statistically tied,” not that A is ahead. A small headline gap between two candidates whose intervals overlap is consistent with either one actually leading.

Example 5.6. Reading the poll headline.

A national poll of 1,067 likely voters reports that 45% favor a new policy. Compute the approximate margin of error at 95% confidence, write the 95% confidence interval, and interpret the result.

Solution.

Step 1. Apply the rule of thumb. MOE ≈ 1/√n = 1/√1067 ≈ 1/32.7 ≈ 0.0306, or about 3%.

Step 2. Build the interval. The 95% confidence interval is p̂ ± MOE = 45% ± 3% = [42%, 48%].

Step 3. Interpret. We are 95% confident that between 42% and 48% of all likely voters favor the policy — or more carefully, the method used to produce this interval has a 95% long-run success rate at containing the true population proportion. A second poll on the same population could plausibly produce a sample proportion anywhere in this range; differences within the interval should not be over-interpreted.

Notice the symmetry between the prose of the poll headline (“45%, ±3%”) and the structure of the math: the headline number is the sample proportion, the ± is the margin of error, and the implicit confidence level is 95%.

In the wildPolitical polls publish their margin of error in fine print at the bottom of the report. National polling organizations such as Gallup, Pew Research, and the major newspapers' polling units typically sample between 1,000 and 2,500 likely voters, producing margins of error in the 2–3% range. Larger surveys (the U.S. Census, for example) sample millions of households and have margins of error well under 1% for most measurements. The same machinery underwrites confidence intervals in clinical trials (where p̂ is the success rate of a new drug), market research (where p̂ is a customer-preference rate), and public health (where p̂ is the prevalence of a condition).

content.problem: A poll of **1,500 likely voters** finds 48% support for Proposition A. Estimate the **95% margin of error** using the rule of thumb **1/√n**.
To halve the margin of error from ±2.6% to ±1.3%, how many likely voters must be polled in total? How many more than the original 1,500?

content.text:
1. Compute the approximate margin of error at 95% confidence for a poll of 400 people.

    Show answer

    Answer. About 5%. 1/√400 = 1/20 = 0.05.

  2. A survey of 2,500 customers finds that 62% are satisfied. What is the approximate margin of error, and what is the 95% confidence interval?

    Show answer

    Answer. MOE ≈ 1/√2500 = 1/50 = 0.02 = 2%. The 95% confidence interval is 62% ± 2% = [60%, 64%].

  3. If you wish to cut the margin of error of a poll in half, by what factor must you multiply the sample size?

    Show answer

    Answer. 4. Because MOE ≈ 1/√n, halving the MOE requires multiplying n by 4 (the square of 2).

  4. True or false: a 95% confidence interval guarantees that the true population proportion lies inside the computed interval.

    Show answer

    Answer. False. The 95% refers to the long-run success rate of the procedure, not to a probability about the specific interval computed from one sample. In practice, the distinction matters more in research statistics than in everyday reading.

  5. Candidate A polls at 50%, ±3%; Candidate B polls at 47%, ±3%. Is A statistically ahead of B?

    Show answer

    Answer. Not at 95% confidence. The two intervals are [47, 53] for A and [44, 50] for B; they overlap. The result is consistent with either candidate actually leading; statisticians would call the race “within the margin of error.”

  6. A pollster wishes to estimate a population proportion to within ±1%. What sample size n is needed at 95% confidence?

    Show answer

    Answer. About 10,000. Setting 1/√n = 0.01 and solving gives n = 1/(0.01)2 = 10,000.

content.label: Margin of error — rule of thumb (95% CI, proportion)
Margin of error — full formula (95% CI, proportion)

content.title: The 1/√n rule of thumb
content.problem: A political poll of **1,000 likely voters** finds that **47%** support a measure. What is the approximate **95% margin of error**?
content.solution:
**Use the rule of thumb MoE ≈ 1/√n for a proportion.**

√1,000 ≈ 31.6.

MoE ≈ 1/31.6 ≈ **0.032 = ±3.2%**.

So the 95% confidence interval for true support is roughly **47% ± 3.2%**, or between about **43.8% and 50.2%**.

*Important framing.* The ±3.2% is in *percentage points*, not relative percent. The true support is between 43.8 and 50.2, not between 47 × 0.968 and 47 × 1.032.

*The full formula* is MoE = 1.96 × √(p(1−p)/n). For p near 0.5, that simplifies to about 0.98/√n ≈ 1/√n.

content.title: To halve the margin, quadruple the sample
content.problem: A poll uses **n = 1,000** and reports MoE ≈ **±3%**. To tighten the margin to **±1.5%**, how many additional voters must be sampled?
content.solution:
**Use the inverse-square-root relationship.**

Halving MoE means doubling √n, which means **quadrupling n**.

New required n = 4 × 1,000 = **4,000 voters**.

Additional voters needed = 4,000 − 1,000 = **3,000 more**.

*The takeaway.* MoE shrinks **slowly** as you sample more. Doubling the sample (1,000 → 2,000) only reduces MoE by a factor of √2 ≈ 0.71 — going from ±3% to ±2.1%. Cutting MoE in *half* requires four times the sample. This is why political polls almost always sit around n = 800–1,200 with MoE ≈ ±3%: the cost of going much beyond that grows quadratically while the precision improvement grows only modestly.

content.title: Summary
content.content: Margin of error answers the question "how close is our sample-based estimate to the population truth?" For a 95% confidence interval on a proportion, the rough rule MoE ≈ 1/√n is good enough for most uses. The cost of halving MoE is quadrupling the sample size — a brutal scaling law that limits how precise polls can practically be.

content.title: Margin of error in political headlines
content.content: Look closely at any election poll: "49% support Candidate X, ±3%." That ±3% is what makes the poll honest. With a margin of error of ±3%, two candidates polling 48% and 51% are statistically *tied* — neither lead is outside the noise. Cable news talking heads regularly miss this: a "3-point lead" with a 3-point MoE means we genuinely don't know who's ahead. Knowing how to read MoE means knowing when to call an election close vs called, and when to discount a confident-sounding headline as actually uncertain.

content.text: This chapter closes the descriptive and introductory inferential side of statistics.
